teamObjective.explanation: My hand is very weak (only low non-trump clubs), so the best team plan is to lead the cheapest card and let partner try to win the trick, rather than pretend strength.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 cards and has not acted yet. They may have a mid-to-high single or a trump that can beat this low lead, but I cannot assume any specific cards.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards and is near finishing; they are likely to hold at least one playable card that can beat a low single, though the exact ranks and suits are hidden. Player 3 has 3 cards and may support them.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the club 3 is the lowest-risk opening: it spends the weakest card, keeps the slightly stronger 4-6 for later, and gives partner the easiest possible chance to take control while forcing opponents to use a higher card if they want the lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is stronger than the 3 but still weak; playing it now wastes a card that might be useful later and does not materially increase the chance of winning the trick.
The 5 is the second-strongest card in my hand; leading it would reveal a slightly higher card prematurely and leave me with weaker follow-ups.
The 6 is my best card and might only win if everyone else passes, which is unlikely; committing it now would strip me of any future high-card option without improving team control.

risks: Player 1 could beat the 3 with a higher single and then use their remaining card to finish.
Partner may be unable to win the trick, leaving opponents in control.
I have no trumps, so I cannot recover the lead once it is lost.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By playing the absolute lowest card, I minimize the value surrendered if opponents win and preserve my slightly higher clubs for later. I also make it as cheap as possible for partner to cover and take over the lead.
